children.name: Glareola
children.description: Pratincoles — elegant, term-like waders with long pointed wings and a forked tail. They catch insects on the wing like swallows, flying with a buoyant, swooping action. Their short legs reflect their aerial lifestyle. Nests on open ground in dry plains and salt pans. The name comes from Italian 'pratincola' meaning 'meadow-dweller'.

children.name: Glareola pratincola
children.description: A graceful, term-like wader of southern Europe, Africa, and Asia. Brown above, white belly, and a distinctive cream-coloured throat bordered by a black collar. In flight, the white rump and forked tail are conspicuous. Nests in colonies on dry open ground near water. Feeds on flying insects over wetlands and grasslands.

children.name: Glareola nordmanni
children.description: Very similar to the Collared Pratincole but with black underwings (not chestnut) and a shorter tail. Breeds in the steppes of Ukraine, Russia, and Kazakhstan. Winters in southern Africa. A regular vagrant to western Europe, including Sweden. Classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Glareola maldivarum
children.description: The Asian pratincole, breeding from India through Southeast Asia to China and wintering in Indonesia and Australia. Olive-brown above, chestnut underwing. Migratory flocks often gather in huge numbers at traditional stopover sites.

children.name: Glareola ocularis
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar, where it breeds on inland lakes and rivers, migrating to East African coasts after breeding. Slaty-grey above, white below, with a distinctive white eye-stripe and red bill base.

children.name: Cursorius
children.description: Coursers — fast-running waders of arid plains, named for their speed across the ground. Unlike pratincoles, they rarely fly and prefer to run from danger, relying on their superb camouflage. Their elongated hind claws provide stability on loose sand. Found across Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia.

children.name: Cursorius cursor
children.description: A pale, sandy-coloured wader of the Sahara, Middle East, and the Canary Islands. Cream-buff plumage perfectly matches desert sand. It runs rapidly on long legs, stopping to pick insects from the ground. A rare but regular vagrant to southern Europe.

children.name: Cursorius temminckii
children.description: Found across sub-Saharan Africa in dry savanna and open woodland. Warm brown above, paler below, with a distinctive white supercilium and a chestnut nape patch. Named after the Dutch ornithologist Coenraad Jacob Temminck.
